text.fullText: February 17, 2026 Submitted via: http://www.regulations.gov U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, EPA Docket Center, Water Docket, Mail Code 28221T 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Re: Docket ID No. EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 – Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Dear Madam or Sir: The Petroleum Alliance of Oklahoma (The Alliance) strongly supports the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA’s) proposed rule published on January 15, 2026, regarding Docket ID No. EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 – Updating the Water Quality Certification (WQC) Regulations (Proposed Rule). The Alliance provides the following comments and recommendations for your consideration. The Alliance is the largest oil and gas trade association in the Mid-Continent and the only trade association in Oklahoma to represent all sectors of the state’s oil and natural gas industry. Representing more than 1,700 individuals and member companies, the Alliance’s membership includes oil and natural gas producers, service providers to the oil and natural gas industry, midstream companies, refiners, and other associated businesses. Our members include companies of all sizes, ranging from small, family- owned companies to large, publicly traded corporations. Our members are responsible for 83% of all operated crude oil and natural gas production in Oklahoma. When non-operated production is considered, we estimate our members produce, transport, process, and refine more than 97% of Oklahoma’s crude oil and natural gas. Additionally, our members have operations, assets, or interests in most of the United States’ oil and natural gas producing regions as well as internationally. Our members develop private, state, and federal minerals and operate on federal lands in Oklahoma and in other states. The Proposed Rule is important to our members, especially those with large linear projects that cross multiple states that require a federal permit from the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers under Section 404 of the Clean Water Act (CWA). Some states have delayed or denied such permits for a myriad of issues unrelated to water quality. The Proposed Rule Increases Transparency, Efficiency, and Predictability for Section 401 Certifications. EPA’s Proposed Rule is a vital step toward restoring the rule of law and providing the regulatory certainty required for long-term capital investment for large infrastructure projects. The Proposed Rule balances the scope of the CWA Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule (2023 Rule) to 500 N.E. 4th Street, Oklahoma City, OK 73104 405-942-2334 www.thepetroleumalliance.com 1 provide greater regulatory certainty for applicants to receive their WQCs for projects while protecting waters of the U.S. The Proposed Rule aligns the regulations with the best reading of the CWA Section 401 and the legislative history, increases transparency, efficiency, and predictability for certifying authorities and the regulated community, and ensures that States and authorized Tribes understand and adhere to their Section 401 role. Among the many proposed revisions supported by The Alliance, the Proposed Rule better aligns the regulation with the law, focuses on the “discharges” rather than the “activity”, appropriately and reasonably aligns Section 401 certifications with the purpose of the CWA, establishes a "reasonable period of time" for state review (i.e., not to exceed one year in accordance with the law), clarifies when and how the 1-year clock for certification is started, limits the certifying authorities request for additional information not related to the discharge, identifies specific content in certification decisions and explanation of certification conditions, prohibits states from requesting an applicant to withdraw and resubmit a request for certification, eliminates automatic extensions, and removes Section 121.11 and instead appropriately directs Tribes to utilize the existing regulations at 40 CFR 131.8 if they are interested in pursuing treatment in a similar manner as a State for CWA Section 401. Recommendations to Enhance Clarity, Predictability and Consistency of the Proposed Rule 1. Information or Conditions Exceeding Section 401. If a state requests information from an applicant or imposes conditions in a WQC that exceed (i.e., is outside) the scope of the Section 401 identified in the Proposed Rule, applicants would have to remedy the issue in court. This means litigation of both the Proposed Rule and individual WQC is likely to continue. EPA is responsible for developing regulations and guidance to ensure effective implementation of CWA Section 401. To minimize or avoid unnecessary litigation, we request EPA include text in the final rule that provides an illustrative list of prohibited non-water quality information or conditions exceeding (i.e., are outside the scope) Section 401, and explicitly prohibit requests or allow conditions that are non-water quality related in the final rule. 2. Extension of the Reasonable Period of Time. Section 121.6(d) states that, [t]he Federal agency and certifying authority may agree in writing to extend the reasonable period of time for any reason (emphasis added), provided that the extension shall not cause the reasonable period of time to exceed one year from the date that the request for certification was received.” We support this statement except for the inclusion of “any reason” as it could be used as a tactic to delay a WQC up to the 1-year statutory limit unnecessarily. Such extensions should only be considered where the certifying authority has provided a credible justification regarding a significant issue related to the project discharge, not just “any reason” and without any justification. In addition, the applicant should be provided notice and justification of the extension. As such, we request EPA include language in the final rule that requires the certifying authority provide credible justifications for an extension of the reasonable period of time that are substantial to the project discharge to ensure the process is not unreasonably delayed. Additional text should be included in the final rule requiring the justification and notice of the extension to be provided to the applicant. 3. Modification. We support the inclusion of language “that the Federal agency, the certifying authority, and applicant agree in writing that the certifying authority may modify a grant of certification (with or without conditions), the certifying authority may modify only the agreed-upon portions of the certification” and that “[t]he certifying authority is required to 500 N.E. 4th Street, Oklahoma City, OK 73104 405-942-2334 www.thepetroleumalliance.com 2 obtain the applicant’s agreement on the language of the modification.” However, the proposed revisions do not identify the level of significance in which a modification can be considered and/or initiated. For example, modifications should only be considered where there is a "significant change" in the discharge, i.e., one that materially increases the volume or changes the chemical nature of the discharge. Minor design revisions that do not affect the “discharge” should not result in a modification. We request EPA include language in the final rule to address that a modification can only be considered and/or initiated when there is a significant change in the discharge. 4. Enforcement. The Proposed Rule does not provide clarity on what entity conducts compliance inspections and enforcement actions. This creates significant regulatory uncertainty for our members. Since a specific Federal agency issues a permit, it is only reasonable and appropriate for that Federal agency to conduct compliance inspections and enforcement. Otherwise, having multiple entities [state(s) and the Federal agency] conducting compliance inspections and enforcement will only lead to regulatory uncertainty and inconsistent inspections and enforcement, especially if there are multiple certifying authorities involved. As such, we request EPA add language to the final rule that the authority for compliance inspections and enforcement actions lies with the Federal agency issuing the permit. 5. “Other State”. Under Section 121.14(d), “[t]he Federal agency shall consider recommendations from the notified other State and the Administrator, and any additional evidence presented to the Federal agency at the hearing, and determine whether additional Federal license or permit conditions may be necessary to ensure that any discharge from the project will comply with the other State’s water quality requirements. If such conditions may be necessary, the Federal agency shall include them in the Federal license or permit.” However, this provision does not provide a timeframe in which the Federal agency makes this determination. We request EPA include text in the final rule that requires the Federal agency to make this determination as soon as possible, but no later than 30 days ensuring that the determination does not exceed the statutory 1-year certification time frame. Conclusion The Section 401 certification process must be transparent, predictable, and grounded in the law. By streamlining the certification process, it allows our industry to more efficiently plan and construct critical infrastructure required to provide Americans with affordable, reliable energy. This not only lowers costs for American consumers but also strengthens our ability to support our allies abroad. The Alliance requests EPA move quickly to finalize the Proposed Rule. We appreciate your consideration of the above comments and recommendations to enhance regulatory certainty of the final rule. If you have any questions, please contact me at 405-601-2124. Sincerely, Angie Burckhalter Sr. V.P. of Regulatory and Environmental Affairs 500 N.E. 4th Street, Oklahoma City, OK 73104 405-942-2334 www.thepetroleumalliance.com 3
